title-group.article-title: Prevalence of suicidal ideation, suicide plan, and suicide attempt among firefighters: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aStudent Research Committee, Kerman University of Medical Sciences, Kerman, Iran
bDepartment of Health Economics and Management Sciences, School of Health, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
cDepartment of Psychiatry, Psychotherapy and Psychotraumatology, Bundeswehr Hospital Berlin, Berlin, Germany
dDepartment of Medical Emergencies and Health in Disasters and Emergencies, Ilam University of Medical Sciences, Ilam, Iran
eNon-Communicable Diseases Research Center, Ilam University of Medical Sciences, Ilam, Iran

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Medical Emergencies and Health in Disasters and Emergencies, Ilam University of Medical Sciences, Ilam, Iran. ali.sahebi.phd@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: The profession of firefighting is one of the most dangerous and stressful occupations, and firefighters are at greater risk of suicide due to the experience of unfortunate incidents. This systematic review and meta-analysis study aimed to investigate the overall prevalence of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt among firefighters.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: The present study is a systematic review and Meta-Analysis.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The study followed PRISMA guidelines. The protocol of this study was registered in PROSPERO with the code CRD42023469261. Data sources including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were used to identify and extract related studies. Searches were conducted without a time limit until the end of July 2023. The random effects model was used for meta-analysis, and the I2 index was used to check heterogeneity between studies. Data were analyzed using STATA software (version 14).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In this study, 2003 primary articles were identified from the information sources. After the screening, study selection, and quality assessment, 21 articles entered the meta-analysis stage, and 86,989 firefighters were examined. Based on the results of the meta-analysis, the overall prevalence of suicide plan, attempt, and ideation among firefighters was reported as 17.91 % (95 % CI = 7.72.-28.10, I2 = 99.5 %, p < 0.001), 24.01 % (95 % CI = 14.92–33.09, I2 = 99.9 %, p < 0.001), and 28.29 % (95 % CI = 24.35–32.22, I2 = 99.7 %, p < 0.001), respectively. The I2 index obtained in all three cases showed very high heterogeneity between the investigated studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The results of this study showed that firefighters are at relatively high risk of suicide plans, attempts, and ideation. Therefore, it is vital to take necessary measures to prevent suicide among them. It is suggested that firefighters be periodically screened for mental disorders and receive essential training in the field of stress management and increasing psychological resilience.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Firefighting is recognized as one of the most demanding and high-risk occupations, requiring personnel to respond to emergencies, disasters, and critical incidents under extreme physical and psychological stress [1]. Firefighters routinely face life-threatening situations, exposure to severe injuries or death, unpredictable work schedules, long and irregular shifts, and the responsibility of making life-or-death decisions. Such occupational stressors can result in adverse mental health outcomes, including Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), depression, anxiety, and substance misuse [2,3]. The cumulative effect of these stressors not only affects overall well-being but also increases vulnerability to suicidal ideation, planning, and attempts, making suicide a pressing concern in this population [4].

Suicide is a complex and multifaceted public health problem, encompassing a spectrum from suicidal thoughts to planning and attempts, with profound individual, social, and economic consequences [5,6]. Suicidal ideation represents the initial stage in the progression of suicidal behavior and may manifest through impulsive or premeditated acts, potentially culminating in suicide attempts or completed suicide [6]. Early identification of suicidal thoughts and behaviors is critical to prevent fatal outcomes and to design effective intervention strategies.

Suicidal ideation represents the initial stage in the progression of suicidal behavior and may manifest through impulsive or premeditated acts, potentially culminating in suicide attempts or completed suicide [6]. Early identification of suicidal thoughts and behaviors is critical to prevent fatal outcomes and to design effective intervention strategies [7].
Existing research indicates that firefighters are at elevated risk compared to both the general population and other occupational groups [[8], [9], [10], [11]].

For instance, among 1027 active and retired firefighters, 46.8 % reported experiencing suicidal ideation, 19.2 % had formulated suicide plans, and 15.5 % had attempted suicide during their careers [9]. In contrast, general population estimates show suicidal ideation ranging from 5.6 % to 14.3 %, suicide planning around 4 %, and suicide attempts between 2 % and 9 % [12]. Moreover, firefighters exposed to the suicide of a colleague face a 3.5-fold increased risk of suicidal behavior themselves [13]. These findings highlight both the occupational specificity and the severity of suicide risk in this group [14].

Although some studies have examined suicidal ideation, planning, and attempts individually, there remains a lack of comprehensive synthesis of prevalence estimates in firefighters. Additionally, variations in study design, survey instruments, sample size, and reporting practices have contributed to inconsistencies in the literature, limiting our understanding of the true burden of suicidal behavior in this population. A systematic review and meta-analysis can integrate existing evidence, quantify the overall prevalence, and provide insights into trends, risk factors, and potential moderators.

Comparative analyses with other populations further emphasize the heightened risk among firefighters. Studies indicate lower prevalences of suicidal behavior in other occupational groups: agricultural, forestry, and fisheries workers (1.48 %) [15]; construction workers (1.25 %) [16]; transport workers (1.32 %) [17]; and nurses and midwives, who also show elevated but lower rates than firefighters [18]. Similarly, general population studies reveal considerably lower lifetime prevalences of suicidal ideation and attempts: 3.9 % and 0.8 % in China [19], 10.7 % among Chinese students [20], 8.0 % in Germany [21], and 14 % among adolescents globally [22]. These comparisons underscore the disproportionate burden of suicidal behavior in firefighting and highlight the need for occupation-specific interventions.

Understanding suicidal behavior in firefighters is critical not only for individual and public health but also for organizational and policy planning. Comprehensive knowledge of prevalence rates, occupational risk factors, and demographic influences can guide the development of targeted mental health programs, stress management interventions, peer support initiatives, and policy-level recognition of firefighting as a high-risk profession. By systematically synthesizing existing evidence, this study aims to inform strategies to reduce suicide risk, enhance occupational well-being, and improve the effectiveness of emergency response services.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: The current study was a systematic review and meta-analysis conducted in accordance with PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) [23], and the study protocol was registered in PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews) with the registration number CRD42023469261.

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p:
In this study, to search and extract related studies, Data sources including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were used. To formulate search strategies for various databases of valid keywords including "Fire and Rescue Personnel∗", "Fire Fighter∗", "Fire Official∗", "Firefighter∗", "Suicide∗", "Suicidal ideation", “Suicidal thought∗”, “Suicide attempt∗”, “Suicidal behavior∗”, search fields and operators were used. Searches were conducted without a time limit until the end of July 2023. Table 1 shows the search strategy in different types of databases.Table 1
Search strategy in various types of databases.Table 1
DatabaseSearch strategy
Pubmed((“Fire and Rescue Personnel∗” OR “Fire Fighter∗” OR “Fire Official∗” OR “Firefighter∗”) AND (“Suicide∗” OR “Suicide ideation” OR “Suicide thought∗” OR “Suicide attempt∗” OR “Suicide behavior∗”))
Scopus(((ALL(“Fire and Rescue Personnel∗”) OR ALL(“Fire Fighter∗”) OR ALL(“Fire Official∗”) OR ALL(“Firefighter∗”)) AND (ALL(“Suicide∗”) OR ALL(“Suicide ideation”) OR ALL(“Suicide thought∗”) OR ALL(“Suicide attempt∗”) OR ALL(“Suicide behavior∗”))))
ISI(((TS=(“Fire and Rescue Personnel∗”) OR TS=(“Fire Fighter∗”) OR TS=(“Fire Official∗”) OR TS=(“Firefighter∗”)) AND (TS=(“Suicide∗”) OR TS=(“Suicide ideation”) OR TS=(“Suicide thought∗”) OR TS=(“Suicide attempt∗”) OR TS=(“Suicide behavior∗”))))

sec.title: Inclusion criteria
sec.p: All studies that reported the prevalence of suicide ideation, planning, and attempt among firefighters by the end of July 2023 were included.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Interventional studies, surveys, case reports, and letters to the editor on the prevalence of suicide ideation, planning, and attempt among occupations other than firefighters were considered as exclusion criteria.

sec.title: Selection of studies
sec.p: Considering that it is necessary to manage the searched articles in the first stage of the review study, all the studies obtained from the search in information sources were entered into the bibliographic management software EndNote version 9. Then duplicates were removed. In the next step, the titles and abstracts of other articles were carefully examined and screened. Potentially relevant articles were reviewed by two researchers independently (ST and AT). Any disagreement between the two researchers was resolved by a third individual (SG). Finally, after carefully reviewing the articles and taking into account the inclusion and exclusion criteria, 21 studies were selected for qualitative evaluation.

sec.title: Quality assessment and data extraction
sec.p: At this stage, there was a need to check the quality of the extracted studies. Therefore, the Appraisal tool for Cross-Sectional Studies (AXIS) was used to evaluate the quality of each study [24]. This tool has a score range of 0–20, and the articles with a score of 12 or higher were selected for meta-analysis. At this stage, two researchers (SG and FM) collected data and evaluated the quality of the articles. It should be noted that each of the researchers evaluated each article separately. The data extracted by them included: the name of the first author, the size of the study sample, and the prevalence of suicide ideation, planning, and attempt. These extracted data were entered into a pre-prepared checklist. Any disagreement between the two evaluators was resolved by a third evaluator (AT).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: In each study, the prevalence of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt, and the sample size were extracted. Then, binomial distribution was used to calculate the variance of each study at this stage. Also, a weighted average was used to combine the prevalence of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt in the reviewed studies and each study was weighted based on its inverse variance. In this study, a simple random effects model was used to perform meta-analysis. It should be noted that to check the level of heterogeneity between studies, the I2 index was calculated for all three prevalences of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt. Heterogeneities between less than 25 %, 50-25 %, 50–75 %, and more than 75 % were zero, moderate, high, and very high, respectively [25]. To check publication bias, Begg's test was used for all three cases. Also, meta-regression was performed separately based on the year of publication of the studies conducted in the field of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt. The data of the present study were analyzed using STATA (version 14).

Meta-regression analyses were planned to explore potential sources of heterogeneity. However, due to inconsistent reporting across the primary studies, information on participants’ age, gender, and years of service could not be extracted in a comparable form. Similarly, survey instruments were diverse but insufficiently reported to allow reliable subgroup analysis. Sample size was available but was already accounted for in study weighting within the meta-analysis. Therefore, publication year was the only moderator consistently reported and was included in the meta-regression.

sec.title: Systematic review results
sec.p:
In this review study, 2003 studies were identified after conducting an initial search in databases. Then, duplicates were removed, and 1822 studies entered into the screening stage. After screening, 72 articles were selected for full-text review. At this stage, by investigating the references of reviewed articles and their citations, 21 studies were selected for quality assessment. Finally, these 21 studies entered into the meta-analysis stage (Fig. 1). In the current study, 86,989 firefighters were investigated in terms of the prevalence of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt. Cross-sectional methodology was used in all selected studies (Table 2).Fig. 1
Flowchart of study selection based on PRISMA guideline.Fig. 1Table 2
The Specifications of Studies Included in the Meta analysis.Table 2
First authorCountrySample sizeThe prevalence of suicide ideationThe prevalence of suicide attemptThe prevalence of suicide planSurvey instrument∗Group study
Park (2019) [26]South Korea4569810.66 %NR∗∗NRA self-reported questionnaire, SBQ-R, PHQ-9Korean firefighters
Noor (2019) [27]USAMen: 7529 %N = 22NRNRPHQ-9, DSM-IVU.S female firefighters
Women: 256415 %N = 395NRNR
Chu (2016) [28]USA863NR91.8 %NRACSS-FAD, SITBI-SF, DSI-SS,U.S Firefighters
Stanley (2015) [9]USA102746.8 %15.5 %19.2 %SITBI-SFCurrent and retired firefighters
Hom (2016) [29]USA48368 %93 %77 %National Web-based survey, SITBI-SF, self-report survey administeredFirefighters
Gallyer (2018) [30]USA24140.2 %NRNRSITBI-SFU.S. women firefighters
Stanley (2017) [8]USA31337.7 %3.5 %10.9 %Self-report questionnaire, SITBI-SFU.S. women firefighters
Barros (2013) [31]Brazil30315 %NRNRSSIfirefighters
Bartlett (2018) [32]USA76524.4 %NRNRSBQ-RFirefighters
Kimbrel (2016) [11]USA6141 %NR8 %SBQ-Rthree different U.S. fire departments
Hom (2017) [33]USA29037.9 %3.8 %10.3 %SITBI-SF, SBQ-RWomen Firefighters
Hom (2017) [34]USA92945.9 %14.6 %18.3 %SITBI-SF, SBQ-R,Firefighters
Hyun (2020) [35]Korea181012.7 %NRNROnline-based self-report questionnaireFirefighters
Martin (2017) [2]USA30369.1 %0.7 %NRSelf-administrated questionnairefirefighter/EMS personnel of HFD
Hom (2018) [36]USA16040.0 %5.0 %13.1 %SITBI-SF, ACSS-FADwomen firefighters
Stanley (2020) [37]USA917NR14.3 %NRACSS-FAD, PPES, SITBI-SF, SBQ-RUS firefighters
Almeida (2022) [38]Brazil6582NR(2018)28.5 %NRTBI, GCSFirefighters
Almeida (2022) [38]Brazil6582NR(2019)35.0 %NRTBI, GCSFirefighters
Almeida (2022) [38]Brazil6582NR(2020)36.5 %NRTBI, GCSFirefighters
Serrano (2020) [39]USA865NR1.4 %NRSBQ-R, FFMQFirefighters
Stanley (2017) [40]USA52544.0 %15.8 %17.9 %SITBI-SF, DSI–SS, SBQ-Rvolunteer and career firefighters
Streeb (2019) [41]USA2168.3 %NR1.85 %FFCSE, CSE-TFirefighters
Kyron (2021) [42]Australia29756.9 %0.7 %2.6 %NSMHWBFire and rescue
ACSS-FAD: Acquired Capability for Suicide Scale-Fearlessness about Death, SBQ-R: suicidal Behavior Questionnaire-Revised, PHQ-9: nine-item Patient Health Questionnaire, SITBI-SF: Self-Injurious Thoughts and Behaviors Interview-Short Form, DSI-SS,: Depressive Symptom Inventory-Suicidality Subscale, SSI: Scales for measuring current suicide ideation, PPES: Painful and Provocative Events Scale, TBI: traumatic brain injury, GCS: Glasgow Coma Scale, FFMQ: Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire, CSE-T: self-efficacy for trauma, NSMHWB: National Survey of Mental Health and Wellbeing, ∗∗NR: Not Reported.

sec.title: Meta-analysis results
sec.p:
Based on the results, the prevalence rate of suicide plan among firefighters was reported as 17.91 % (95 % CI = 7.72.-28.10, I2 = 99.5 %, p < 0.001), the prevalence rate of suicide attempt among firefighters was 24.01 % (95 % CI = 14.92–33.09, I2 = 99.9 %, p < 0.001), and the prevalence of suicidal ideation among firefighters was reported as 28.29 % (95 % CI = 24.35–32.22, I2 = 99.7 %, p < 0.001). The I2 index obtained in all three cases shows a very high heterogeneity between the investigated studies (Fig. 2, Fig. 3, Fig. 4).Fig. 2
The prevalence of suicide plan and 95 % confidence interval for each of the studied studies and all the studies conducted.Fig. 2Fig. 3
The prevalence of suicide ideation and the 95 % confidence interval for each of the studied studies and all the studies conducted.Fig. 3Fig. 4
The prevalence of suicide attempts and the 95 % confidence interval for each of the studied studies and all the studies conducted.Fig. 4

The results of Begg's test indicated that publication bias was not significant for any of the outcomes: suicidal ideation (p = 0.705), suicide plan (p = 1.000), and suicide attempt (p = 0.00.235) (Fig. 5).Fig. 5
Funnel plots assessing publication bias for the prevalence of suicidal behavior among firefighters. Begg's test results indicate no significant publication bias for any outcome: suicidal ideation (p = 0.705), suicide plan (p = 1.000), and suicide attempt (p = 0.235).Fig. 5

sec.title: Meta-regression results
sec.p:
Meta-regression was conducted using publication year as the sole moderator, given that other variables such as participants’ age, gender, years of service, and survey instruments were not consistently reported in the included studies. The results indicated a decreasing trend between publication year and the prevalence of suicide ideation, plan, and attempt (Fig. 6).Fig. 6
Meta-regression based on the year of the study (suicide ideation, suicide Plan, and (suicide attempt).Fig. 6

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 studies demonstrated that firefighters face alarmingly high prevalences of suicidal ideation (28.29 %), suicide plans (17.91 %), and suicide attempts (24.01 %). These rates are substantially higher than those observed in the general population and in many other occupational groups. Such findings underscore the urgent need to recognize firefighting as a high-risk profession for suicidal behaviors.

The elevated prevalence among firefighters can be explained by their unique occupational stressors. Unlike many other professions, firefighters are repeatedly exposed to life-threatening situations, traumatic scenes involving death and severe injury, unpredictable emergency calls, and physically demanding work under extreme conditions. Long and irregular shifts, disrupted sleep cycles, and the responsibility of making life-or-death decisions under pressure contribute further to psychological strain. Over time, these stressors may result in chronic stress, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), depression, anxiety, and substance misuse—all of which are well-established risk factors for suicidal ideation and behavior [43,44]. These job-related exposures help clarify what is meant by “high stress and psychological consequences” in the context of firefighters.

When compared to other occupational groups, the elevated risk among firefighters becomes clear. Suicidal ideation has been reported at 11 % among military personnel [45] and 17 % among physicians, with suicide attempts at 1 % [46]. Among public safety personnel in Canada, suicidal ideation, suicide plans, and attempts were 25.2 %, 8.8 %, and 3.3 %, respectively [47,48]. In contrast, the rates we observed for firefighters were consistently higher, particularly for suicide plans and attempts. Other occupational comparisons further illustrate the relative risk: agricultural, forestry, and fisheries workers had a suicidal behavior prevalence of 1.48 % [15]; construction industry workers, 1.25 % [16]; transport workers, 1.32 % [17]; and nurses and midwives also showed significant prevalence [18].

General population studies show much lower estimates. In China, lifetime prevalences of suicidal ideation and attempts were 3.9 % and 0.8 %, respectively [19]; among Chinese students, suicidal ideation was 10.72 % [20]; in Germany, 8.0 % [21]; and a global study across 82 countries reported 14 % among adolescents [22]. Taken together, these findings confirm that firefighters experience a disproportionate burden compared to both general populations and other professions.

The high heterogeneity observed across studies (I2 > 99 %) likely reflects methodological variability, including different survey instruments, inconsistent reporting of demographics, and variations in sample size. Although our meta-regression examined publication year as the only moderator consistently available, other factors—such as age, gender, and years of service—are also likely to influence prevalence but were insufficiently reported. The decreasing trend over time may point to improved awareness, access to support services, or methodological changes in recent research, though residual heterogeneity warrants caution.

Beyond prevalence comparisons, several studies have provided insight into risk factors underlying suicide in firefighters. Occupational exposures, particularly psychosocial stressors, are strongly associated with suicide [43,44]. A comparative study of high-risk occupations (firefighting, military, and law enforcement) found that PTSD, substance use, job problems, and intimate partner issues were common correlates of suicide across groups [49]. Anxiety sensitivity has been identified as a mediator between PTSD symptoms and suicide risk among female firefighters [50]. In a study of 483 current and retired US firefighters with suicidal ideation or attempts, 77 % had used mental health services [29], underscoring both the severity of the problem and the demand for support.

Other studies have highlighted additional vulnerabilities: firefighters’ higher risk compared to other occupational groups is reflected in lifetime suicide threats and current suicidal intent [51]. Epidemiological research has shown that a greater proportion of deceased firefighters had pre-existing physical health conditions [52], and reduced physical activity has also been linked to higher suicide prevalence [53,54]. These findings reinforce the multifactorial nature of suicide risk in firefighters, involving both psychological and physical health domains.

From a policy and practice perspective, the findings have significant implications. Suicide prevention among firefighters requires comprehensive and tailored interventions, including.•
Routine mental health screening to identify at-risk individuals early.•
Resilience and stress-management programs integrated into firefighter training.•
Confidential, stigma-free access to psychological support services and peer support.•
Organizational measures such as adequate rest periods, structured decompression after critical incidents, and improved shift scheduling.•
Policy-level recognition of firefighting as a high-risk occupation to ensure sustained funding for mental health initiatives.

By addressing both individual-level and organizational-level risk factors, these policies could reduce suicide risk, improve mental health outcomes, and enhance the effectiveness of fire and rescue services during disasters.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: Although additional moderators such as participants’ age, gender, years of service, and survey instruments could potentially influence the prevalence of suicidal ideation, plan, and attempt, these variables were inconsistently reported across the included studies, limiting our ability to include them in the meta-regression. Sample size was available but already incorporated in the weighting of studies in the meta-analysis. Consequently, publication year was the only moderator consistently available and included in the meta-regression. The observed decreasing trend over time may reflect improvements in occupational mental health awareness, the implementation of stress management programs, increased access to psychological support services for firefighters, or methodological differences in more recent studies. However, given the high residual heterogeneity, these interpretations should be made cautiously, highlighting the need for future studies to systematically report demographic and methodological details to enable more comprehensive moderator analyses.

Despite these limitations, the study offers two notable strengths. First, it is the first systematic review and meta-analysis to integrate existing evidence on suicidal ideation, suicide planning, and suicide attempts among firefighters, thereby addressing a critical yet understudied public health concern. Second, through the use of a comprehensive search strategy and a rigorous methodological framework, the study delivers a robust and reliable synthesis that can inform future research directions and support the development of targeted preventive interventions for this high-risk occupational group.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 studies demonstrated that firefighters face elevated prevalences of suicidal ideation (28.29 %), suicide plans (17.91 %), and suicide attempts (24.01 %), rates markedly higher than those reported in the general population and many other occupational groups. These findings highlight the unique occupational stressors firefighters encounter, including repeated exposure to traumatic events, physical strain, irregular shifts, and high-responsibility decision-making under pressure, which may contribute to increased suicide risk.

Rather than attributing suicidal behaviors solely to mental disorders, our results emphasize the need for comprehensive, evidence-based preventive strategies. These include routine monitoring of psychosocial risk factors, access to timely psychological support, resilience-building programs, and organizational measures such as adequate rest, decompression after critical incidents, and improved shift scheduling. Tailored policies and interventions are essential to safeguard firefighters’ mental health and enhance the effectiveness of fire and rescue services.

sec.title: Ethics approval
sec.p: The study was approved by Kerman University of Medical Sciences, Kerman, Iran (ethical code: IR.KMU.REC.1404.390).

sec.title: Consent for publication
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Availability of data and materials
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: AS and ST managed the project. AS, ST, AT, and SG developed the inclusion criteria, screened titles, and abstracts. SG, FM, AT, and ST read and screened articles for inclusion. SG, FM, AT, and ST performed the qualitative assessment and data extraction. AS as a statistician and re-checked statistical analysis. ST, UW, and AT were a major contributor in writing the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Declarations of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: The authors of this study would like to thank !!!
